text: We present the case of a fourteen-year-old female with a one-year history of diffuse abdominal pain causing vomiting that worsened two weeks prior to being seen in our emergency department.
Her medical history was significant for depression.
On physical examination, a mass was palpable from the pigastrium to the umbilical area.
CT scan revealed a mass that was contained in the stomach.
This mass was characteristic of a trichobesaur.
During laparoscopy, the surgeon identifies a rigid stomach due to the presence of a mass within the gastric lumen.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac to mobilize the stomach, preparing for laparoscopic surgery.
The surgical team measures the size of the stomach to tailor a containment bag, aiming to prevent contamination of the peritoneal cavity during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform a gastrostomy, making an incision to open the stomach.
The laparoscopic view reveals a thin gastric wall and the visible components of the trichobezoar within the gastric lumen.
The surgeon measures the gastrostomy, noting its extension to be approximately ten centimeters.
The surgeon carefully peels off the stomach wall, attempting to pull the bezoar into a specimen bag to prevent contamination.
Most important component of doing this is avoiding, as much as possible, injuring the stomach wall.
This patient had a Rapunzel syndrome that is characterized by a tail of the bezoar traveling to the small bowel.
The surgeon uses gentle traction to reduce and draw the tail of the bezoar into the gastric cavity, visibly maneuvering the mass within the stomach.
It is very important to be able to reduce this tail in its totality.
There are cases reported where this besoar tail can travel down to the ileocecal valve.
The specimen is placed inside a retrieval bag, which is then closed to secure the contents.
retrieved through a funnestal incision.
After our colorectal experience, we consider that these type of incisions are ideal in terms of pain and even cosmesis.
The surgeon uses round clamps to remove the specimen from the surgical site.
You can see the abdomen of the patient.
The surgeon closes the funnestal incision, washes the area, and then proceeds to close the gastrotomy.
The surgeon uses a monofilament absorbable suture in a running fashion to close the gastrotomy.
The surgical team closes the incision in a single layer, following their routine practice.
The stomach will be next tested for Ehrlich
The operative time was eighty minutes.
The patient had an uneventful post-operative course, was able to be discharged in post-op day four.
Trichobesolar is an atypical cause of gastric outlet obstruction.
It needs to be suspected in patients with psychiatric disorders.
A large trichobesolar can be managed laparoscopically with minimal contamination.
A financial incision is always a good option to remove a large specimen with good cosmetic results.
